title: Polymorphism & Abstract
subtitle: One word, many forms — let your objects wear different masks.
goal: Understand how a parent-type variable can hold any child object, how the right overridden method runs automatically, and how abstract classes force children to fill in the blanks.

blocks.heading: What is polymorphism? 🎭
blocks.html:
Polymorphism is a big word that means “many forms.” In Java it means one variable can take on many shapes depending on what you put inside it.

Imagine a box labeled Animal. You can drop a Dog in it, or a Cat, or a Cow — the box still says Animal, but what's inside decides how it behaves. 🐶🐱🐮

blocks.heading: A parent box holding a child
blocks.html:
If Dog extends Animal, then this line is perfectly legal:

Animal a = new Dog();

The left side is the box type (Animal). The right side is the real object (a Dog). When you call a.speak(), Java looks at the real object and runs the Dog's version. This is called dynamic method dispatch — a fancy name for “the overridden method runs.”

blocks.caption: A parent variable, a child object
blocks.explain: Even though a and b are declared as Animal, Java runs the Dog and Cat versions of speak(). The real object decides which method runs — not the box label.

blocks.html: Remember: the variable type (left side) is the box; the object (right side) is what's inside. The object always wins when deciding which overridden method to run.

blocks.heading: Treating many objects the same way 🔁
blocks.html: The real magic is that you can put lots of different children into one parent array and loop over them with the same code. You don't need to know exactly what each one is — you just say speak() and each object does its own thing.

blocks.caption: One loop, many different sounds
blocks.explain: The array is an Animal[], but it holds a Dog, a Cat, and a Cow. The loop calls speak() once per animal, and each one answers in its own voice. One loop, three behaviors — that's polymorphism in action.

blocks.heading: Abstract classes: a promise to fill in later 📝
blocks.html:
Sometimes a parent class doesn't make sense on its own. What is the “area” of a plain Shape? You can't say — it depends on whether it's a circle or a square.

So we mark the class abstract. An abstract class cannot be created directly (no new Shape()). It can have abstract methods — methods with no body, just a promise. Any child must fill them in, or the code won't compile.

blocks.caption: Abstract Shape with Circle and Square
blocks.explain: Shape promises an area() but doesn't define it. Circle and Square each keep the promise with their own formula. You can store both in a Shape[] and ask for area() without caring which shape it really is.

blocks.html: Heads up: writing new Shape() on an abstract class is an error — Java won't let you build something that has unfinished (abstract) methods. You can only build the finished children.

blocks.question: What does Animal a = new Dog(); a.speak(); print, if Dog overrides speak() to say "Woof!"?
options: An error, because a is an Animal
Both the Animal and Dog versions
blocks.explain: The real object is a Dog, so Java runs the Dog's overridden speak(). That's dynamic method dispatch — the object wins, not the box label.

blocks.question: Why do we make a class abstract?
options: So it can be created with new many times
Because it's incomplete on its own and forces children to fill in certain methods
To hide it from other files
blocks.explain: An abstract class represents an idea that isn't complete by itself. It cannot be instantiated, and its abstract methods must be implemented by each child.

blocks.question: What's the benefit of putting different child objects in one Animal[] array?
options: You can loop over them with the same code and each behaves in its own way
It deletes the children when done
It turns them all into the same animal
blocks.explain: A parent array lets you treat many objects uniformly. One loop, one method call, but each object responds with its own overridden behavior.

blocks.title: Give the parrot a voice
blocks.instructions: A Parrot is an Animal, but right now its speak() just prints ___. Change the text inside Parrot's speak() so it prints Squawk! Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Squawk! The parrot found its voice — that's an overridden method running through a parent variable.
blocks.failMsg: Keep pet declared as Animal, and make Parrot's speak() print exactly Squawk! inside the quotes.

blocks.title: Finish the abstract Shape
blocks.instructions: Rectangle extends the abstract Shape but its area() returns 0.0 — wrong! Make area() return width * height so the program prints Area: 20.0.
blocks.passMsg: Perfect — Rectangle kept the promise made by abstract Shape, and 4 * 5 gives 20.0.
blocks.failMsg: Inside Rectangle's area(), return width * height instead of 0.0.

challenge.title: Build a one-line band 🥁🎸🎹
challenge.instructions:
Make an abstract class Instrument with an abstract method play(). Create three children — Drum, Guitar, and Piano — that each override play() to print their own sound. Put all three in an Instrument[] array and loop over it so the program prints:
Boom boom!
Twang!
Plink plink!
challenge.hint: Each instrument needs its own class that extends Instrument and overrides play(). Then write { new Drum(), new Guitar(), new Piano() } in the array so the loop plays all three.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 The whole band plays from one loop — that's polymorphism plus abstract classes working together. Day 14 done!
